Wellness Center </h1>\n                                                                                  </div>\n                                        <div id="content-body" class="row-fluid clearfix" about="/projects/auxiliary-buildings/gallo-recreation-and-wellness-center" typeof="sioc:Item foaf:Document">      <span property="dc:title" content="Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center" class="field-item even"><p class="p1"><img alt="" height="543" src="https://campusmap.ucmercedhub.com/files/images/recreationwellness.jpg" width="565"></p>\n<p class="p1"><span><span><strong>The Joseph Edward Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center</strong>&nbsp;is the home of the campus recreation and health and wellness programs. It features a gymnasium with 500 bleacher seats and a full-size NCAA basketball court that can be configured in various ways to accommodate two cross-courts for basketball as well as volleyball and badminton courts.&nbsp;</span></span></p>\n<p class="p1"><span>The recreation center also provides state-of-the-art cardio and fitness equipment, and a group exercise room with equipment.&nbsp; The recreation and athletics program sponsors a number of club sports and activities such as hikes and whitewater rafting excursions.</span></p>\n<p><span><strong>The H. Rajender Reddy Student Health Center</strong>&nbsp;occupies the second floor and serves the health and wellness needs of the campus community, working with other campus departments and faculty to offer a range of wellness activities, from student peer health counseling and nutrition programs to massage therapy.</span></p>\n<h3><span><span><strong>Project Details</strong></span></span></h3>\n<p class="p1"><span>Size:&nbsp;35,400 square feet</span><br>\n\t<span>Construction Cost:&nbsp;$9 million</span><br>\n\t<span>Completion Date:&nbsp;August 2006</span><br>\n\t<span>Awards:&nbsp;LEED-NC Gold</span><br>\n\t<span>Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA</span><br>\n\t<span>Project Director: Michael Chow, AIA</span><br>\n\t<span>LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell</span><br>\n\t<span>Architect &amp; Engineers: Sasaki Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise</span><br>\n\t<span>Contractor: Howard S. Wright &nbsp;Construction Company&nbsp;</span></p>\n<h3><span><span><strong>Building Highlights</strong></span></span></h3>\n<ul>\n<li><span>This building has exceeded California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beats the LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>This building achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building through the use of automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>Overall the building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n</ul>\n<ul>\n<li><span><span>Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used in the project have low volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and meet a number of low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.</span></span></li>\n</ul>\n</div></div></div>  </div>\n    </article> </div>\n</div></div>\n <!-- /.region -->\n James Smith and Thomas E. Wright &nbsp;Construction Company&nbsp;</span></p>\n<h3><span><span><strong>Building Highlights</strong></span></span></h3>\n<ul>\n<li><span>This building has exceeded California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beats the LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>This building achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building through the use of automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>Overall the building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n</ul>\n<ul>\n<li><span><span>Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used in the project have low volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and meet a number of low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.</span></span></li>\n</ul>\n</div></div></div>  </div>\n    </article> </div>\n</div></div>\n <!-- /.region -->\n  </div>\n                                                                                                  </div>', 'tags': ['parking', 'places', 'transit'], 'type': 'polygon', 'start': '', 'end': ''}, 'search_blob': 'Joseph E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA</span><br>\n\t<span>Project Director: Michael Chow, AIA</span><br>\n\t<span>LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell</span><br>\n\t<span>Architect &amp; Engineers: Sasaki Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise</span><br>\n\t<span>Contractor: Howard S. Wright &nbsp;Construction Company&nbsp;</span></p>\n<h3><span><span><strong>Building Highlights</strong></span></span></h3>\n<ul>\n<li><span>This building has exceeded California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beats the LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>This building achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building through the use of automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>Overall the building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span>55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.<br>\n\t\t&nbsp;</span></li>\n</ul>\n<ul>\n<li><span><span>Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used in the project have low volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and meet a number of low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.</span></span></li>\n</ul>\n</div></div></div>  </div>\n    </article> </div>\n</div></div>\n <!-- /.region -->\n line-height: 15.432px;">&nbsp;</h3>\n<p><strong>Sierra Terraces</strong>&nbsp;was UC Merced\'s second campus housing project and includes Tuolumne and Mariposa Halls, dormitory buildings that together house 400 students, primarily freshmen. Dormitory rooms can accommodate up to three students if necessary, making the total potential capacity of the building up to 600 beds. The buildings include lounges, meeting rooms/study spaces, and a kitchen in each building for student use.&nbsp;</p>\n<h3><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Project Details</span></h3>\n<p><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Size:&nbsp;84,500 square feet</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Construction Cost:&nbsp;$24 million</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Completion Date:&nbsp;August 2007</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Awards:&nbsp;LEED-NC Gold</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Project Director: Catherine Kniazewycz, AIA</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Architect &amp; Engineers: Fisher Friedman Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise</span><br>\n\t<span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Construction Managers: The Hallmark Group and&nbsp;ProWest Constructors</span></p>\n<p>&nbsp;</p>\n<h3><strong>Sustainability Awards</strong></h3>\n<p>American Society of Heating, Refrigerating, and Air-Conditioning Engineers: Technology Awards, Energy Savings Dorms (2010)</p>\n<p>&nbsp;</p>\n<h3><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Project Highlights</span></h3>\n<ul>\n<li>The project has surpassed California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 42% and beats the LEED energy performance baseline by 44%, &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; achieving 9 points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.&nbsp;</li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">90% of construction waste was diverted from the landfill.&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Building materials had a combined value of 21% recycled content, earning 2 basic points plus a third point under Innovation and Design Credit for &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; Exemplary Performance.&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">49% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one basic point plus an additional point under Innovation and Design Credit for &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; Exemplary Performance.</span></li>\n</ul>\n</div></div></div>  </div>\n    </article> </div>\n</div></div>\n <!-- /.region -->\n  </div>\n                                                                                                  </div>', 'geometry': {'rings': [[[-13405980.656423979, 4490097.608385045], [-13406000.44675989, 4490118.407948586], [-13405988.729289154, 4490129.556869163], [-13405990.297927985, 4490131.332041801], [-13405975.454618372, 4490145.575626161], [-13405973.48358193, 4490143.464181585], [-13405965.814698022, 4490150.823213886], [-13405967.258460606, 4490152.304862691], [-13405943.345406309, 4490175.805330361], [-13405941.646951359, 4490173.883249452], [-13405935.068715444, 4490180.076429325], [-13405936.767170394, 4490181.998510234], [-13405923.861737046, 4490194.148525996], [-13405922.003242444, 4490192.217704507], [-13405910.648067506, 4490203.1140878815], [-13405890.775520328, 4490182.228123622], [-13405902.432826294, 4490171.253176298], [-13405900.501656769, 4490169.184438583], [-13405913.422287881, 4490157.020114656], [-13405915.42388544, 4490159.28525125], [-13405921.809314232, 4490153.273592566], [-13405919.622289095, 4490151.001455144], [-13405943.741006803, 4490127.588177514], [-13405945.944356998, 4490129.903889388], [-13405953.70708242, 4490122.980250973], [-13405951.665189646, 4490120.834230174], [-13405966.774662629, 4490106.609187906], [-13405968.923324905, 4490108.867423007], [-13405980.656423979, 4490097.608385045]]], 'spatialReference': {'wkid': 3857}, 'type': 'polygon'}, 'id': 'polygon1761860798659497', 'image_urls': ['https://campusmap.ucmercedhub.com/files/images/sierra.jpg'], 'label': {'hideAfter': None, 'hideBefore': 14, 'name': 'Mariposa'}, 'location_id': 1362, 'sections': [{'bullets': ['Size: 84,500 square feet', 'Construction Cost: $24 million', 'Completion Date: August 2007', 'Awards: LEED-NC Gold', 'Campus Architects: E. Heating, Refrigerating, and Air-Conditioning Engineers: Technology Awards, Energy Savings Dorms (2010)</p>\n<p>&nbsp;</p>\n<h3><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Project Highlights</span></h3>\n<ul>\n<li>The project has surpassed California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 42% and beats the LEED energy performance baseline by 44%, &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; achieving 9 points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.&nbsp;</li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">90% of construction waste was diverted from the landfill.&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">Building materials had a combined value of 21% recycled content, earning 2 basic points plus a third point under Innovation and Design Credit for &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; Exemplary Performance.&nbsp;</span></li>\n<li><span style="line-height: 1.538em;">49% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one basic point plus an additional point under Innovation and Design Credit for &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; Exemplary Performance.</span></li>\n</ul>\n</div></div></div>  </div>\n    </article> Gallo Recreation & Wellness Center",\n      "subtitle": "",\n      "host": "",\n      "description": "<div id=\\"content-wrapper\\" class=\\"content-wrapper\\">\\n          <div id=\\"content-head\\" class=\\"row-fluid content-head\\">\\n                                                <h1 class=\\"title\\" id=\\"page-title\\"> Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center </h1>\\n                                                                                  </div>\\n                                        <div id=\\"content-body\\" class=\\"row-fluid content-body\\"> <div class=\\"region region-content clearfix\\">\\n  <div id=\\"block-system-main\\" class=\\"clearfix block block-system\\">       <div class=\\"content\\"> <article id=\\"node-31\\" class=\\"node node-page clearfix\\" about=\\"/projects/auxiliary-buildings/gallo-recreation-and-wellness-center\\" typeof=\\"sioc:Item foaf:Document\\">      <span property=\\"dc:title\\" content=\\"Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center\\" class=\\"rdf-meta element-hidden\\"></span>    <div class=\\"content\\">\\n    <div class=\\"field field-name-field-page-body field-type-text-long field-label-hidden\\"><div class=\\"field-items\\"><div class=\\"field-item even\\"><p class=\\"p1\\"><img alt=\\"\\" height=\\"543\\" src=\\"https://campusmap.ucmercedhub.com/files/images/recreationwellness.jpg\\" width=\\"565\\"></p>\\n<p class=\\"p1\\"><span><span><strong>The Joseph Edward Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center</strong>&nbsp;is the home of the campus recreation and health and wellness programs. It features a gymnasium with 500 bleacher seats and a full-size NCAA basketball court that can be configured in various ways to accommodate two cross-courts for basketball as well as volleyball and badminton courts.&nbsp;</span></span></p>\\n<p class=\\"p1\\"><span>The recreation center also provides state-of-the-art cardio and fitness equipment, and a group exercise room with equipment.&nbsp; The recreation and athletics program sponsors a number of club sports and activities such as hikes and whitewater rafting excursions.</span></p>\\n<p><span><strong>The H. Rajender Reddy Student Health Center</strong>&nbsp;occupies the second floor and serves the health and wellness needs of the campus community, working with other campus departments and faculty to offer a range of wellness activities, from student peer health counseling and nutrition programs to massage therapy.</span></p>\\n<h3><span><span><strong>Project Details</strong></span></span></h3>\\n<p class=\\"p1\\"><span>Size:&nbsp;35,400 square feet</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Construction Cost:&nbsp;$9 million</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Completion Date:&nbsp;August 2006</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Awards:&nbsp;LEED-NC Gold</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Project Director: Michael Chow, AIA</span><br>\\n\\t<span>LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Architect &amp; Engineers: Sasaki Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise</span><br>\\n\\t<span>Contractor: Howard S. Wright &nbsp;Construction Company&nbsp;</span></p>\\n<h3><span><span><strong>Building Highlights</strong></span></span></h3>\\n<ul>\\n<li><span>This building has exceeded California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beats the LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.<br>\\n\\t\\t&nbsp;</span></li>\\n<li><span>This building achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building through the use of automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.<br>\\n\\t\\t&nbsp;</span></li>\\n<li><span>Overall the building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.<br>\\n\\t\\t&nbsp;</span></li>\\n<li><span>55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.<br>\\n\\t\\t&nbsp;</span></li>\\n</ul>\\n<ul>\\n<li><span><span>Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used in the project have low volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and meet a number of low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.</span></span></li>\\n</ul>\\n</div></div></div>  </div>\\n    </article> </div>\\n</div></div>\\n <!-- /.region If you're interested in wildlife on campus, you might want to check out the "Cute bunny" sighting at the New Pavilion Lawn, which highlights a recent encounter with a bunny. Additionally, the "Spring 2026 Camera Trapping" event organized by the Paleontology and Life Organization involves deploying cameras in natural grassland habitats to document wildlife around Merced, providing a more structured opportunity to observe campus wildlife.
